summary: My name is Peter Brooks, born in Washington, D.C., into a fortunate family with a famous grandfather. I earned my undergraduate degree from McDaniel College and a master's from New York University. Over the years, I worked as a college professor and filmmaker, and now I teach stress reduction classes for veterans in Maryland. I consider myself a helper, visionary, activist, and spiritual person, always striving to make a difference.

I was born in Washington, D.C., which shaped much of my early life and identity. Growing up there, I was fortunate to have access to a good education and opportunities. The city is part of my roots and the backdrop to my family's history and my own journey.

I earned my master's degree from New York University, which was an important step in my educational journey. This advanced degree helped me in my career as a college professor and filmmaker. NYU represents a key part of my academic background and professional development.

I completed my undergraduate studies at McDaniel College, which laid the foundation for my later academic and professional pursuits. My time there was crucial in shaping my early education and preparing me for graduate school at NYU. McDaniel College is where I began to develop my vision and activism.

Currently, I live and work in Maryland, where I teach stress reduction classes for veterans. Maryland is where I help people chill out and contribute to the community, especially veterans. It’s also part of my family’s history, as my father moved to this area around the time of the Great Depression.

The election of Donald Trump was a very difficult time for my family and me. We were traumatized and couldn’t believe the American people would elect him and sustain his presidency for so long. As a strong anti-Trump left person, I was constantly communicating information and felt the political climate deeply affected my activism and personal life during the pandemic.

During the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, the CPAC conference was happening, and some executives I worked with had attended it. I noticed one of them sounded sick, which made me panic about potential exposure to the virus. This event was part of what triggered my decision to isolate and eventually not return to my job in senior living.

Facebook reminded me of my stepfather’s newspaper publishing days, as it was like his dream come true for reaching audiences efficiently. I saw Facebook as a powerful tool for activism and social change during my generation’s time. During the pandemic, I used Facebook to communicate and organize, recognizing its role in shaping information and social interaction.

My dad fought in World War II, volunteering because he felt it was his generation’s responsibility. His service during that era had a big impact on me and my sense of duty. I see my own generation’s challenge as leveraging modern tools like Facebook to make a difference, much like my father did through his service.

My father moved to the Maryland and Washington area around the time of the Great Depression, which shaped much of our family’s history. The economic hardships of that era influenced how I view struggles and resilience. I often reflect on that time when thinking about current crises like the pandemic and economic challenges for people of color.

The COVID-19 pandemic was a turning point for me, forcing me to isolate after potential exposure and rethink my life and legacy. It was a time of fear, anxiety, and social disruption, compounded by political tensions and economic struggles. The pandemic pushed me to reinvent myself, focus on activism, and use tools like Facebook to try to make a meaningful impact.
